title: Configuration Management
summary: Profiles, externalized configuration, Spring Cloud Config Server, Vault integration, and configuration best practices.

sections.content:
## Externalized Configuration

Spring Boot loads configuration from multiple sources with a strict **priority order** (higher overrides lower):

1. Command-line arguments (`--server.port=9090`)
2. `SPRING_APPLICATION_JSON` environment variable
3. OS environment variables
4. `application-{profile}.properties` outside jar
5. `application.properties` outside jar
6. `application-{profile}.properties` inside jar
7. `application.properties` inside jar
8. `@PropertySource` annotations
9. Default properties

sections.title: Configuration Priority Order
sections.content:
graph TD
    CLI["1. CLI args\n--server.port=9090"] --> ENV
    ENV["2-3. Env vars\nSERVER_PORT=9090"] --> EXT
    EXT["4-5. External files\napplication.properties (outside jar)"] --> INT
    INT["6-7. Internal files\napplication.properties (inside jar)"] --> DEF
    DEF["8-9. Defaults\n@PropertySource, hardcoded"]
    style CLI fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style DEF fill:#6b7280,color:#fff

## Profiles

Profiles activate different configuration sets per environment:

```yaml
# application.yml — base config
app:
  name: MyApp

---
# application-dev.yml
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: dev
spring:
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:h2:mem:devdb

---
# application-prod.yml
spring:
  config:
    activate:
      on-profile: prod
spring:
  datasource:
    url: ${DB_URL}  # from environment
```

Activate via:
```bash
# Environment variable (recommended for containers)
SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod java -jar app.jar

# JVM argument
java -Dspring.profiles.active=prod -jar app.jar
```

## Spring Cloud Config Server

For microservices, centralize configuration in a Config Server backed by Git:

```yaml
# Config Server application.yml
spring:
  cloud:
    config:
      server:
        git:
          uri: https://github.com/org/config-repo
          default-label: main
```

```yaml
# Client application.yml
spring:
  config:
    import: configserver:http://config-server:8888
  application:
    name: order-service  # loads order-service.yml from git repo
```

Refresh config at runtime without restart:
```java
@RefreshScope  // bean re-created when /actuator/refresh is called
@RestController
public class ConfigController {
    @Value("${feature.enabled}")
    private boolean featureEnabled;
}
```

sections.title: Spring Cloud Config Architecture
sections.content:
graph LR
    Git[Git Repo\nconfig files] -->|pull| CS[Config Server]
    CS -->|serve config| S1[Order Service]
    CS -->|serve config| S2[Payment Service]
    CS -->|serve config| S3[User Service]
    Bus[Spring Cloud Bus\nRabbitMQ/Kafka] -->|refresh event| S1
    Bus --> S2
    Bus --> S3
    Admin[Admin] -->|POST /actuator/busrefresh| CS
    CS --> Bus
    style CS fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style Git fill:#059669,color:#fff

sections.title: Never commit secrets to config repositories
sections.content: Use Spring Cloud Vault or AWS Secrets Manager for sensitive values. Config Server can integrate with Vault: spring.cloud.config.server.vault.* properties let Config Server fetch secrets from Vault and serve them to clients.

sections.question: What is the highest-priority configuration source in Spring Boot?
sections.explanation: Command-line arguments (--property=value) have the highest priority in Spring Boot's property resolution order. This makes it easy to override any configuration at deployment time without changing files.
